words: This fifth conversation with ARTHUR SUNDBERG takes place at his home in Potlatch,
Idaho on August 7, 1975.
SAM SCHRAGER: I've heard that Corliss engine was really a monster.

speaker: AS
words: Yeah. They had two of fem. They had one that drove the sawmill and

speaker: AS
words: they had one that drove the planing mill. Course, the one that drove

speaker: AS
words: the sawmill was half again larger than the one that drove the planer.

speaker: AS
words: But the one that drove the sawmill, they kept it in place down there

speaker: AS
words: after it was taken out of service for a few years. And there was a

speaker: AS
words: lot of different discussions as to what to do with it. And the old

speaker: AS
words: Jew scrapiron dealer, he wanted it for scrap, because it was the highest

speaker: AS
words: quality scrap that you could .find. But he wanted to buy it for

speaker: AS
words: so much a ton on board a car. Well, management approached me on it

speaker: AS
words: and I discouraged them on that because they wouldn't only give the

speaker: AS
words: engine away but they'd lose money too, because it would cost more

speaker: AS
words: money to take it out of there than it would to- for what they'd get

speaker: AS
words: for it for scrap. So it just set there. Well, then finally a man

speaker: AS
words: by the name of Williams up here in Spokane, he's a brother to this

speaker: AS
words: Williams that makes this time and temperature signs, you know. You've

speaker: AS
words: seen these electric signs that change every minute, gives you the

speaker: AS
words: temperatures. He talked them into giving it to him, but he would take

speaker: AS
words: it out, that is, he would pay for taking it out. And he was going to

speaker: AS
words: put it up , up I Spokane, it s out there by Medical Lake along with

speaker: AS
words: a lot of other old sawmill, mining and farm equipment and make a mu

speaker: AS
words: seum, you know, for the public. So they give it to him. And so he

speaker: AS
words: approached me, wanted me to take it out. That was after I'd retired.

speaker: AS
words: And I told him, no way would I take it out, because I said I don't

speaker: AS
words: do any lifting or slugging or any of that heavy work.

speaker: AS
words: Well, he said

speaker: AS
words: he didn't want me to do any work, he said he'd get ironworkers from

speaker: AS
words: Spokane to do the work but he wanted me to supervise it. You know,

speaker: AS
words: to tell them how to take it apart and then mark all the pi eces so

speaker: AS
words: they could be reassembled. So I agreed to that. So I supervised

speaker: AS
words: the taking the engine down and shipping it to Spokane. But, oh, that

speaker: AS
words: thing,it run from the time the mill started- and I'm not entirely

speaker: AS
words: certain whether that was a brand new engine when it was installed here.

speaker: AS
words: See, that was built by that Minneapolis Company, and it seems to me

speaker: AS
words: like there was only three engines that was ever built ^that size. And

speaker: AS
words: the other two I believe, was installed in big breweries back in Wis

speaker: AS
words: consin, and there's one in Minneapolis and I think there's one in

speaker: AS
words: Wisconsin. But at the time that that was dismantled they'd discon^

speaker: AS
words: n^cted the big steam pipe. There was a twelve inch steam line that

speaker: AS
words: supplied the engine, but they disconnected that and just hooked up

speaker: AS
words: a two inch airline to it. And there wasn*t any load on it, you know,

speaker: AS
words: so periodically when they had visitors coming through, a bunch of

speaker: AS
words: visitors, why then they'd start up the engine and let them see it run.

speaker: AS
words: It was just run on air pressure but no load. But that thing run

speaker: AS
words: just like a jeweled watch. Well, person really should have a pic

speaker: AS
words: ture of it to see- to get some idea of the size of it. The flywheel

speaker: AS
words: on it was sixty inches across and twenty-four feet in diameter, And

speaker: AS
words: the spokes on that wheel, you know, the spokes were about like so

speaker: AS
words: you know. Course, they weren*t round, they were kind of flat and it

speaker: AS
words: was all castiron. The frame was all castiron. And the only thing

speaker: AS
words: that was steel was the connecting rod and of course the bearings^

speaker: AS
words: with babbitt, the inserts. But they had a forty-eight cylinderforty-

speaker: AS
words: eight inches in diameter cylinder,. And gee, I forget the stroke

speaker: AS
words: on it, but anyway it was 1,200 horsepower in one engine. So that was

speaker: AS
words: quite a piece of machinery.

speaker: SS
words: Would you explain briefly how it worked? The Corliss engine?

speaker: AS
words: Well, it's a little bit difficult to explain unless a person is ac

speaker: AS
words: quainted with engines, stationary engines. I could make you just a

speaker: AS
words: small sketch of the operating mechanism. But anyway, there was ec

speaker: AS
words: centrics on the main crankshaft between the main bearing and the fly

speaker: AS
words: wheel there was two big eccentrics on the shaft there. Now if you

speaker: AS
words: know what an eccentric is- it's something that runs off center. Well,

speaker: AS
words: then connected these eccentrics was pushrods, steel pushrods.

speaker: AS
words: And these rods were about twenty feet long and they d run to

speaker: AS
words: the steamchest. And of course, they worked on the outside of the

speaker: AS
words: steamchest. On the steamchest end of these pushrods- they worked an

speaker: AS
words: eccentric there. On the steamchest, this eccentric just back

speaker: AS
words: and forth- like so, you see- one at each end of this cylinder. Well

speaker: AS
words: then there was rods that come down from that down they opened and

speaker: AS
words: closed valves. Now they had rotary valves that went through. There

speaker: AS
words: was an intake and exhaust valve on each end of this cylinder. And

speaker: AS
words: these rods would open and close these alves, you see, so that when

speaker: AS
words: the intake valve was open on one end the exhaust valve had to be open

speaker: AS
words: on the opposite end, and then vice versa. And that pushed this cylin

speaker: AS
words: der piston back and forth in the cylinder.

speaker: SS
words: How was the steam generated?

speaker: AS
words: Well, from the power plant. They had a battery of boilers there.

speaker: AS
words: They had twelve return flue boilers and then they had one— of course

speaker: AS
words: this big watertube boiler wasn't installed until 192- the beginning

speaker: AS
words: of 1925. But anyway, they burned all their waste from the mill- not

speaker: AS
words: all of it- but they burned whatever waste they needed from the mill

speaker: AS
words: to generate the steam, and then the surplus waste went into a big

speaker: AS
words: burner. This burner was, oh, I don't know the exact measurements

speaker: AS
words: about sixty feet in diameter and I think it was a hundred and ten

speaker: AS
words: feet tall; screen top on it. All the surplus slabs and sawdust and

speaker: AS
words: stuff that they went into this burner and it was burned. That

speaker: AS
words: burner it was torn down, oh, maybe it was in the 1940's. I can*t

speaker: AS
words: give you the exact year. But anyway, they quit burning their sur

speaker: AS
words: plus. And then they hogged all their fuel, that is all the slabs

speaker: AS
words: went through a big hog- knife, hog, that chopped it all up into

speaker: AS
words: small pieces you know and.whatever wasn t burned in the powerplant

speaker: AS
words: they put in a conveyor system and it went across to a big fuel pile

speaker: AS
words: where they could store this fuel where they could bring it back in

speaker: AS
words: and burn it on weekends, holidays and times whenever the mill wasn't

speaker: AS
words: running, you see.

speaker: SS
words: The Corliss engine provided the power for the entire sawmill?

speaker: AS
words: Everything. Everything, that is the entire sawmill plus the greenchain.

speaker: AS
words: I think I told you here before about the belt that they used

speaker: AS
words: on that engine- that went from the engine over into the sawmill.

speaker: AS
words: It took 3000 cowhides to make that one belt. Of course, now when

speaker: AS
words: you say a cowhide, in making a belt they don't use the entire hide,

speaker: AS
words: because the flank and some of those parts^the hide is too soft. They

speaker: AS
words: just use the backs. And they use mostly South American cowhides be

speaker: AS
words: cause they said that they were thicker. This thing was all put toge

speaker: AS
words: ther with glue. And whenever this belt would stretch, which it would

speaker: AS
words: you know after you put on a new belt then when it ru n about a

speaker: AS
words: year, why, then it had stretched to the point where theyld have to

speaker: AS
words: take a piece out of it and reglue it. It takes an experienced man

speaker: AS
words: to do that kind of work. Each layer in thickness of this belt has

speaker: AS
words: to be separated back for a distance of about three feet on each end

speaker: AS
words: of the belt and then each ply has to be separated back and these plies

speaker: AS
words: have to be shaved down to a feather edge, and then they have to be

speaker: AS
words: covered with glue and then these plies would go together like soand

speaker: AS
words: then they had great big wooden clamps that they clamped on that

speaker: AS
words: and they'd stay in these clamps until that glue was dry. And to

speaker: AS
words: make that joint so that it was smooth so that it wouldn't pound as

speaker: AS
words: it went over the pulley, you know, you take on the driven end of

speaker: AS
words: the small pulley it could make quite a racket and it could put a lot

speaker: AS
words: of strain on the pulley itself and on the bearings on the shaft if

speaker: AS
words: it would pound every time it would come over, and of course, the speed

speaker: AS
words: that it would go over it, it would be just like a hammer striking if

speaker: AS
words: it wasn't ci smooth.

speaker: AS
words: So it was skilled work putting a belt

speaker: AS
words: like that together. Well then, it's kind of hard for someone to

speaker: AS
words: picture the transmission of power in a mill like that unless you

speaker: AS
words: could look at some drawings or sketches or something to get some idea.

speaker: AS
words: Now then, this shaft that supported the mainline shaft in the sawmill

speaker: AS
words: where this engine drove onto this mainline shaft, it was seven inches

speaker: AS
words: in diameter. And then of course, it was probably sixty feet long.

speaker: AS
words: But on that shaft were several other pulleys that transmitted the

speaker: AS
words: power to other shafts, great long shafts, and these other shafts would

speaker: AS
words: transmit the power to the main handsaws, you know they had four bandsaws.

speaker: AS
words: Two of them would drive off of one shaft, well then from that

speaker: AS
words: secondary shaft they was- it would drive over onto a third shaft and

speaker: AS
words: of course, each one would as it would progress over it would be a

speaker: AS
words: little smaller in size. But anyway, that mill was 120 feet wide and

speaker: AS
words: about 340 feet long. And when you'd stand on the floor and look.like

speaker: AS
words: that it would look like just a maze of pulleys and shafts and belts

speaker: AS
words: over the entire area. Well, then, you take- Say that the main shaft

speaker: AS
words: runs this way; well then there was another line shaft that run

speaker: AS
words: diagonally to that; ninety degrees to that, that drove the edgers.

speaker: AS
words: Well, get this power off*n this shaft to drive a shaft that was

speaker: AS
words: running ninety degrees to that, that had to be done through some

speaker: AS
words: gears. And those gears, they were called core gears, and these gears

speaker: AS
words: were about five feet in diameter. And they were a castiron hub with

speaker: AS
words: slots in it and these slots they put in wooden teeth, and these teeth

speaker: AS
words: had to be fitted and shaped so that they went in there and then a

speaker: AS
words: would be driven in to tighten that tooth into the casting.

speaker: AS
words: Well

speaker: AS
words: then, you see, you had gear running in this plane another

speaker: AS
words: beveled gear running in this plane and of course they^d match perfectly

speaker: AS
words: together so transmit power from one direction to the

speaker: AS
words: other. Well, then to drive the greenchain, from this main shaft there

speaker: AS
words: was another belt that drove over onto another countershaft and then

speaker: AS
words: of course Nrun in the same plane as this main shaft. But then the

speaker: AS
words: power that went out to the greenchain, it had to run right angles to

speaker: AS
words: that so there had to be another big cord here on that one you see

speaker: AS
words: that would transmit the power out to the- And it's really a stupen

speaker: AS
words: dous piece of engineering. You take the engineering today, you can

speaker: AS
words: set a machine down in any location and at any angle, it doesn't make

speaker: AS
words: any difference how a machine is set down on it's base because you

speaker: AS
words: would attach an electric motor right to it, but when you're going to

speaker: AS
words: run a sawmill as big as this.one was in the earlier years, then run

speaker: AS
words: it off of just one power source, that is really a piece of engineering

speaker: AS
words: That's something that future generations will have no idea whatsoever

speaker: AS
words: of what it was. And it's too bad- I've thought lots of times that

speaker: AS
words: if I had known that a lot of these blueprints were going to be des

speaker: AS
words: troyed and just mutilated and finally just burned, why, it would have

speaker: AS
words: really been something if a person could have kept a lot of them prints,

speaker: SS
words: Was firing the engine, was there must to that, was it a big deal

speaker: SS
words: that had to be done very carefully?

speaker: AS
words: You mean the boilers?

speaker: SS
words: Well, yeah.

speaker: AS
words: You see the engine- you take the four head rigs in the sawmill, they

speaker: AS
words: were all propelled by steam power, and each one of those head rigs

speaker: AS
words: run by a twelve inch castiron cylinder, and these cylinders were

speaker: AS
words: about forty-two feet long, and of course it had a valve on each end

speaker: AS
words: of that, you know to run this piston back and forth and.in turn pull

speaker: AS
words: that carriage back and forth on the track, where it was sawing up the

speaker: AS
words: lumber. Well then each rig had a steam Inigger and that consisted of

speaker: AS
words: two cylinders. One eight inch cylinder and one ten inch cylinder.

speaker: AS
words: You've probably been in a sawmill, have you? Well, you've seen this

speaker: AS
words: thing come up out of the hole and turn the log over? Well, that bar

speaker: AS
words: comes down and it branches out like this; well there's one cylinder

speaker: AS
words: that's attached to this end and one to end, so of course

speaker: AS
words: the different manipulation of these two different cylinders can make

speaker: AS
words: this bar do whatever you want it to do up above here, you see, because

speaker: AS
words: if this one goes up and this one stays put, well then that bars going

speaker: AS
words: to go forward.

speaker: AS
words: Well, if this one goes up the bar's going to go back.

speaker: AS
words: If they both go up, it's going to come straight up. But anyway, there

speaker: AS
words: was one set like that for each headrig. Well then there was a twelve

speaker: AS
words: inch cylinder about a two-foot stroke for each loader, that in turn

speaker: AS
words: tipped the log off. and the log back onto the carriage. Now that was

speaker: AS
words: just for each rig, well, then when logs come into the mill, they

speaker: AS
words: had twelve-inch cylinders there for to kick the logs out of one bull

speaker: AS
words: chain over onto the next bullchain, and then as they go in there they

speaker: AS
words: had another cylinder down below here that kicked the logs onto each

speaker: AS
words: deck. Each carriage had it's own deck of logs ahead so they kicked

speaker: AS
words: them up. So you see, there was steam cylinders all throughout the

speaker: AS
words: mill. All the way from six inches in diameter and twelve-inch stroke

speaker: AS
words: to twelve inch diameter and so on. So it took an awful lot of steam.

speaker: AS
words: Well then, of course, they furnished the steam for part of the town.

speaker: AS
words: The mercantile store and the hotel and the school and the big church

speaker: AS
words: that burned down as well as five houses up on Nob Hill where the brass

speaker: AS
words: lived was all heated with steam from the mill. So then when they

speaker: AS
words: started to build dry kilns; the first dry kiln I think I told you be

speaker: AS
words: fore was an experimental deal. Nobody in the lumber industry knew

speaker: AS
words: anything about drying lumber with dry kilns up until the latter endthe

speaker: AS
words: latter part of the period when we was involved in that First

speaker: AS
words: World War.

speaker: AS
words: And then of course during the wartime the demand for lum

speaker: AS
words: ber went up so high, you know, that it took too long to dry it just

speaker: AS
words: by air, so they started experimenting with drying it with steam. So

speaker: AS
words: then that took more steam, but they had another boiler room at the

speaker: AS
words: planer that drove that Corliss engine and of course that boiler room

speaker: AS
words: is the one that furnished steam for this first dry kiln that

speaker: AS
words: So the boiler room at the sawmill it just furnished steam

speaker: AS
words: for the sawmill and for their pumps and for the town, part of the

speaker: AS
words: town. Well then, in 1924-'25 they'd got to the point where they knew

speaker: AS
words: quite a bit about drying lumber, so they built twenty-four big dry

speaker: AS
words: kilns. And I think I explained those kilns to you before. But anyway,

speaker: AS
words: at that time they connected the steam powerlineTTand at that

speaker: AS
words: time is when they put in this big watertube boiler. The big Kidwell

speaker: AS
words: boiler, and it was- well, I'm not going to quote exactly- but I

speaker: AS
words: think it was about 1200 horsepower boiler, just this one boiler.

speaker: AS
words: Well, they used that then with these other four banks of boilers that

speaker: AS
words: they had in the divisional powerplant. Those original boilers, they

speaker: AS
words: were about five feet in diameter and twenty feet long, something like

speaker: AS
words: that, and they were made up in batteries of three boilers side by

speaker: AS
words: side in each battery and they had four batteries, so there was twelve

speaker: AS
words: of these boilers. You know that was the original setup. And they

speaker: AS
words: were return flue boilers where the water was on the outside of the

speaker: AS
words: flues and the fire from the firebox went to the back end of the fire

speaker: AS
words: box and then come up and come through these flues and then out

speaker: AS
words: the stack.

speaker: AS
words: And these fires- these boilers,they were fired by dutch

speaker: AS
words: ovens instead of- well, the fire was partly under the boiler but

speaker: AS
words: just the backend of the firebox was under the boiler, but anyway,

speaker: AS
words: the firebox was bricked over to where no fire come in contact with

speaker: AS
words: the outer shell of the boiler. And of course, they had this con

speaker: AS
words: veyor system that run from tkfc sawmill through the boiler room. It

speaker: AS
words: carried the fuel; it was the sawdust and ground up slabs, you know,

speaker: AS
words: and then for each boiler they had a metal spout that come down this

speaker: AS
words: conveyor system quite high, it was probably ten feet above the

speaker: AS
words: deck of the boilerroom, and they had metal spouts that was open on

speaker: AS
words: top, just metal troughs, come down into this round firehole;

speaker: AS
words: it was just a casting that was an opening of about twelve or fourteen

speaker: AS
words: ^opening. And in the bottom of this conveyor they had slide gates

speaker: AS
words: that was operated with a long handle, the fireman he'd walk along and

speaker: AS
words: he'd look down in these holes and see how these fires were, you know,

speaker: AS
words: and of course it wouldn't take long to learn just how much fuel you

speaker: AS
words: was supposed to keep in this fire box there, you see, because you're

speaker: AS
words: not supposed to have any exposed grates. And he could adjust these

speaker: AS
words: levers, to adjust the amount of fuel that come down.

speaker: AS
words: Well, now, prior

speaker: AS
words: to 19- the early part of 1925, when they put in this extra big boiler

speaker: AS
words: and built these dry kilns, a man working in the powerhouse, the

speaker: AS
words: fireman, they really had a job cut out for 'em, but still it was

speaker: AS
words: something attractive about it to where- there was for years and years

speaker: AS
words: there that that was twelve hours a day and seven days a week firing

speaker: AS
words: these boilers, and the only time there was ever an opening or vacancy

speaker: AS
words: there was if somebody happened to move out of the country or died,

speaker: AS
words: you know. But, you take during the working hours at the mill, well

speaker: AS
words: these firemen they could just, as I say, adjust these slides and let

speaker: AS
words: the fuel run in there. But then they also had to let some fuel run

speaker: AS
words: out onto the deck and this deck was, oh, about ten foot wide back

speaker: AS
words: from the spouts come down, back to the wall, well they could run fuel

speaker: AS
words: onto this deck, but then when it got up there to where it wouldn't

speaker: AS
words: run out anymore, well, then some of these men had to go up and shovel

speaker: AS
words: it back up against this wall, you know bank up as much as they could

speaker: AS
words: so as to have enough fuel in there on this deck to run 'em the hours

speaker: AS
words: that the mill wasn't running, you know, like on a Sunday or a holi

speaker: AS
words: day or on the few hours during the night, you know, when it wasn't

speaker: AS
words: running.

speaker: AS
words: Well, at that time they had to keep these fires going and

speaker: AS
words: they had to maintain this steam pressure so they had to shovel it in

speaker: AS
words: there. They had these big barley forks, and they would shovel this

speaker: AS
words: fuel? that they d stored there during the day into the hole, so

speaker: AS
words: that run into a lot of hard work. But then after 1925 when they put

speaker: AS
words: in this big boiler they also built a big fuel house so they didn't

speaker: AS
words: have to s tore any more of that fuel on this deck because all the sirplus

speaker: AS
words: fuel then would go - spill,into a conveyor .' ; this powerhouse

speaker: AS
words: conveyor that went at right angles into the fuelhouse and that was

speaker: AS
words: a long conveyor that would distribute that in there. Well then when

speaker: AS
words: ever they needed fuel other than when the sawmill was running, why

speaker: AS
words: then one man would go out in the fuelhouse and one man would stay

speaker: AS
words: in the powerhouse, and there'd be two- there was three men on a

speaker: AS
words: shift. The watertender and two firemen. But this one man would go

speaker: AS
words: out in the powerhouse and he would start that conveyor down there

speaker: AS
words: and then they had doors that would open - the full length of the

speaker: AS
words: building a whole series of doors; each door was about oh six foot

speaker: AS
words: wide, and they could open them up and this fuel*d run into this con

speaker: AS
words: veyor and go back up into the powerhouse, circulate around. And,

speaker: AS
words: of course, whenever the fuel got low in this fuel bin the man out

speaker: AS
words: there had to do quite a bit of breaking down and shoveling to get it

speaker: AS
words: into conveyor. So making steam was more or less complicated and it

speaker: AS
words: took a lot of manpower and a lot of hard work.

speaker: SS
words: You know talking about the power being supplied the town; it makes

speaker: SS
words: me think- I was told that the town, not only the Potlatch supplied

speaker: SS
words: the power to the town, but the town functions, like the school and

speaker: SS
words: different things like that were run by a management. Is that right?

speaker: AS
words: That's right It was a closed school district here, and it was just

speaker: AS
words: for the town. And there was no one outside of the company that paid

speaker: AS
words: any school taxes; the company paid the entire cost. They furnished

speaker: AS
words: the buildings; they paid all the administrative expenses of the

speaker: AS
words: school, so everything was free to the people; to the kids. And we

speaker: AS
words: talked about Mr. Laird before and all during his liTetime.he was always

speaker: AS
words: at the head of the school board and he was for education,

speaker: AS
words: This Potlatch school, as long as it was administered that way, it had

speaker: AS
words: the highest rating in Latah County and the highest rating in the whole

speaker: AS
words: state of Idaho in standing, because this Potlatch School Board, which

speaker: AS
words: was Potlatch Lumber Company, they just hired the very best teachers

speaker: AS
words: that could be found and they maintained a high degree of education

speaker: AS
words: as far as they went, you know it was just a grade school plus a high

speaker: AS
words: school.

speaker: AS
words: So they really had an outstanding school system here all

speaker: AS
words: during that period. I don't know, I think possibly that- what I'm

speaker: AS
words: thinking about probably would carry to the entire nation, but you

speaker: AS
words: know, it used to be that every kid in school was taught penmanship

speaker: AS
words: and all the subjects that they did carry.the kids were really bril

speaker: AS
words: liant in every subject. Now you take right today the average kid

speaker: AS
words: that finishes high school, and I might say probably everyone that

speaker: AS
words: finishes college, you got to have a kind of vivid imagination if

speaker: AS
words: you're even going to read their name! You know they can't write.

speaker: AS
words: Kids use typewriters nowdays, you know, they can't write. And they

speaker: AS
words: can't figure, they've got to use a calculator if they're going to

speaker: AS
words: do any figuring. But it always wasn't that way before got all

speaker: AS
words: these gadgets because you know the kids were taught penmanship; that

speaker: AS
words: was one thing that was pounded into every kid. And another thing too,

words: all the time or if they were just running a boisterous house, you

words: know, or something that disturbed the neighborhood. You can imagine

speaker: AS
words: that any offense that was really to the point of being obnoxious,

speaker: AS
words: well then, they might ask them to leave. But any petty offenses they

speaker: AS
words: never did anything about that.

speaker: SS
words: How effective were their attempts to control liquor in the town?

speaker: AS
words: They didn't make any attempt to control the consumption of liquor.

speaker: AS
words: The only thing is that they just wouldn't permit it to be sold here.

speaker: AS
words: You know they wouldn't allow any saloons in the town. But the men

speaker: AS
words: that worked here, they could bring it in by the suitcase load and

speaker: AS
words: they could ship it in here by express. Now I have seen these great

speaker: AS
words: big steel express cars, you know, off of the mainline, I have seen

speaker: AS
words: as high as three of those coupled together switched onto this line

speaker: AS
words: here and moved in here just all loaded with booze. Most of it would

speaker: AS
words: be fifty gallon barrels of wine, you know, for the Italian community.

speaker: AS
words: But whiskey was shipped in here every week by express, in small pac

speaker: AS
words: kages. But that was just for the individuals consumption; not for

speaker: AS
words: resale. Oh, I suppose if they- if I sent up to Spokane and got four

speaker: AS
words: quarts of whiskey and you wanted one of 'em, I'd sell you one and noc

speaker: AS
words: thing'd be said about that.

speaker: SS
words: So they really didn't keep booze out of town at all? They just dis

speaker: SS
words: couraged a public gathering in saloons.

speaker: AS
words: That's right.

speaker: SS
words: That create the kinds of fights that we talked about back in the

speaker: SS
words: Midwest.

speaker: SS
words: What about the arm of the law? Did they give pretty str ict orders

speaker: SS
words: as far as the—?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah. I think possibly that they had the best relationship here

speaker: AS
words: between the people and the law of any town in the whole United States.

speaker: AS
words: You know, this town superintendent at that time was also the constable.

speaker: AS
words: And of course, he was deputized as a constable through the sheriff

speaker: AS
words: of the county, but of course, the company paid all.his salary. And

speaker: AS
words: the only time that he ever put anyone in jail would be if somebody

speaker: AS
words: would swear out a complaint, why then of course, he would have to

speaker: AS
words: serve that, but generally he would- even if you'd come to him with

speaker: AS
words: a complaint he would try to talk you out of it. He was really a nice

speaker: AS
words: guy. And, oh, I've seen it so darn many times that someone would be

speaker: AS
words: downtown and probably in the poolhall or confectionery and drunker

speaker: AS
words: than a hoot owl and probably couldn't even stand up or maybe he was

speaker: AS
words: just drunk and wanted to fight, like some of 'em get, well, maybe the

speaker: AS
words: proprietor would for Old Harry Gltftve, the constable, and he^d

speaker: AS
words: come and talk to the guy a little bit and then pretty soon he^d look

speaker: AS
words: around and say, "Well, you're a pretty good friend to him, why don't

speaker: AS
words: you take him home?" And that'd be the end of it. And that's the way

speaker: AS
words: it was always run. That is, up until after they sold the town, well,

speaker: AS
words: then of course, they had to have policemen and deputy sheriffs and

speaker: AS
words: patrol cars. And they just imagined, I guess, that crime really got

speaker: AS
words: bad after the town was sold and incorporated, but before that they

speaker: AS
words: didn't need all them law enforcement people. Of course now with all

speaker: AS
words: these beer parlors they got all over the country, why, it really

speaker: AS
words: takes a lot of law enforcement because I guess they really stir up

speaker: AS
words: a mess every night and every weekend, you know,in these beer parlors.

speaker: AS
words: I can't tell you too much about that because I never go in one of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: It's too recent to matter anyway.

speaker: AS
words: Well, you take people that act like that is just disgusting, revolting

speaker: AS
words: to me, I just don't want to be around 'em at all. I've got nothing

speaker: AS
words: against drinking, that is, if a person wants to drink that's fine,

speaker: AS
words: go ahead, but then acting they way they do when they get out into one

speaker: AS
words: of them places. Now they could consume the same amount in their own

speaker: AS
words: home or your home or in a little party, but when they get in to one

speaker: AS
words: of them beer parlors, why then, they really whoop it up] And, I guess

speaker: AS
words: from what I've been told the women are ten times worse than the men!

speaker: SS
words: I wanted to ask you about the Depression here, and what happened

speaker: AS
words: Well, of course, you would have to make some of these responses to

speaker: AS
words: that over a period of time. It wasn't that quick. When they decided

speaker: AS
words: to shut the plant down for the lack of business, well they did notify

speaker: AS
words: the people, they sent out notices that the plant was going down and

speaker: AS
words: of course they notified everybody that was on the job. But they also

speaker: AS
words: notified everybody that they could live in the house there

speaker: AS
words: would be some work, they couldn't promise how much work or what it

speaker: AS
words: would be but there would be some work for a few people. And whatever

speaker: AS
words: work there was to be had it would be prorated —

speaker: AS
words: - so as to help as many people as possible. And for a community,

speaker: AS
words: I believe that we fared better here than the average community anywhere

speaker: AS
words: in the whole country. .There was a few that had a lot of initative,

speaker: AS
words: you know, that would get out and try to improvise and then

speaker: AS
words: of course, there was a few like you'd find anyplace, I guess, that

speaker: AS
words: would just sit on their hands and cry and didn't know what was going

speaker: AS
words: to happen, but then they all weathered it anyway.. At that time most

speaker: AS
words: everybody put in a garden. The company furnished all the ground

speaker: AS
words: that you could possibly want for to put in a garden and they'd give

speaker: AS
words: you any help that they could, you know for putting in a garden. We

speaker: AS
words: were part of one little gardening deal; it wasn't really cooperative

speaker: AS
words: in all respects. We all went together and hired a man to plow up

speaker: AS
words: this piece of ground. And of course, this piece of ground, each one

speaker: AS
words: would draw his own plot as to the ground he wanted to work. But

speaker: AS
words: then as far as working the ground was concerned, why we all went

speaker: AS
words: together and shared the cost of working it up. But^each one of us

speaker: AS
words: then planted their own garden and cultivated and harvested our own

speaker: AS
words: garden. So that helped a whole lot.

speaker: AS
words: And, oh, I can tell you just

speaker: AS
words: from our own experience- for a time there we bought cream from a

speaker: AS
words: farmer out here in the country; he'd bring in about three of these

speaker: AS
words: eight gallon cream cans of sweet cream a week and whatever they'd

speaker: AS
words: pay him per can, for the two cans he'd sell them we'd

speaker: AS
words: pay him that for the one can he'd sell us. Well, of course, eight

speaker: AS
words: gallons of cream would be too much for us so we then in turn would

speaker: AS
words: divide that and my wife's sister and brother-in-law would take half

speaker: AS
words: of it and we'd take half of it. Well, we'd use part of the cream

speaker: AS
words: for cake, you know, ice cream cake, or put it on cereal and then

speaker: AS
words: part of it we'd churn into butter. Made our own butter. Well,

speaker: AS
words: there was- that butter cost us about eighteen cents a pound. Then

speaker: AS
words: there was a farmer over here at Viola that come through town here

speaker: AS
words: peddling and he had cracked wheat. And he was telling about how good

speaker: AS
words: that was for cereal, so I bought, I don't know, seems like it was

speaker: AS
words: about fifteen cents worth andAgot enough to run us

speaker: AS
words: for a year! Wheat was only selling for two bits a bushel. Well,

speaker: AS
words: then I stocked up on- they had a special on this MJB rice, you know

speaker: AS
words: come in packages. So I stocked up on- oh, gosh, I don't know how

speaker: AS
words: much of that. Enough to run us for a year or so.

speaker: AS
words: And then we bought

speaker: AS
words: flour; about four barrels of flour at a time, that was sixteen fifty

speaker: AS
words: pound sacks. And we got a good price on that, I don't know, I guess

speaker: AS
words: a sack of flour was, oh, somewhere around a dollar. I can*t tell

speaker: AS
words: you the exact price. And in our garden we put up about twenty sacks

speaker: AS
words: of spuds and we had several sacks of carrots and rutabegas and we

speaker: AS
words: had lots of cabbage and everything, so we had lots of food. Well,

speaker: AS
words: I went down here at the mill and I made up a few butcher knives and

speaker: AS
words: then I traded some of these knives to a farmer out here for half a

speaker: AS
words: hog. And you know, you can do a lot of things if it's necessary.

speaker: AS
words: But as far as any hardships is concerned we never knew what hardships

speaker: AS
words: were at all. We had more food to eat than we ever had in our life.

speaker: AS
words: And pretty near, might say just about every Saturday night, why,

speaker: AS
words: there'd be a dance up here in this Legion cabin and it would be just

speaker: AS
words: anybody that wanted to sponsor it, it really didn't cost 'em anything

speaker: AS
words: to sponsor it because all you do is probably downtown in the mid

speaker: AS
words: dle of the week and meet somebody and set and talk and say, "Well,

speaker: AS
words: let's have a dance Saturday night up to the cabin." "Okay." So

speaker: AS
words: talk it up and send the word out and the Ladies Aid bring a

speaker: AS
words: a few cookies or a few sandwiches and rustle up some coffee. Then

speaker: AS
words: the music, they had the boys, 'a bunch of brothers. They had

speaker: AS
words: an orchestra and they'd play for whatever they could get. They didn't

speaker: AS
words: have any fixed fee. Well, if you wanted to drop a dime in the hat

speaker: AS
words: or nothing at all or two bits or anything you wanted, you know, that

speaker: AS
words: you felt that you wanted to give; put it in the hat, and that's what

speaker: AS
words: the music got. Everybody socialized and fraternized and had a good

speaker: AS
words: time. Everybody, you know, was in the same boat. But then, just as

speaker: AS
words: soon as times picked up again and people start getting some money,

speaker: AS
words: well then, all this fraternizing kind of died out and then.divided

speaker: AS
words: up into small groups. You'd have two or three friends and I'd have

speaker: AS
words: two or three friends arid this way and that way. But during that

speaker: AS
words: toughest part of the Depression, why, they were altogether in a group.

speaker: SS
words: There was more fraternizing during the Depression than there had been

speaker: SS
words: before?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder if that's why many people when they remember the Depression

speaker: SS
words: don't think of it as such a hardtime because they think of the close

speaker: SS
words: ness of people at that time.

speaker: AS
words: Yes, well, of course, that depends a whole lot on who you're talking

speaker: AS
words: to and the area in which you're talking about. Take the people that

speaker: AS
words: really suffered during the Depression was the people in the cities.

speaker: AS
words: Because in the cities there's no fraternizing at all, and in the ci

speaker: AS
words: ties— Well, I know when they started them CCC camps, well, they started

speaker: AS
words: sending out an awful lot of these young men in the eastern part

speaker: AS
words: of the country out this way. Well, they had several CC camps up

speaker: AS
words: here in the timbered area. And I was in a dentist's office down here

speaker: AS
words: one day just visiting with the dentist, and he was laughing and he

speaker: AS
words: was telling me that they brought a bunch of these CCCs in and of

speaker: AS
words: course they was under the control of the Army. They had a real tough

speaker: AS
words: Army sergeant in every camp, and he had to be tough in order to get

speaker: AS
words: along with some of them guys. But anyway, I guess, I don't know,

speaker: AS
words: the Army doctors or what looked 'em over, but anyway of course, there

speaker: AS
words: was a lot of 'em needed some dental work done and so they brought

speaker: AS
words: three or four truckloads down here to give 'em a little recreation,

speaker: AS
words: and of course there would be several of 'em there that they had to

speaker: AS
words: go into the dentist and get some work done.

speaker: AS
words: And there was one of

speaker: AS
words: these young guys come in and set down in the dental chair there and

speaker: AS
words: he had his foot all bandaged up so the dentist asked him, he said,

speaker: AS
words: "What did you do to your foot? What happened?" "Well," he said,

speaker: AS
words: "I'll tell you; you know, back where we come from we heard a lot

speaker: AS
words: about this wild west out here and the Indians and everything and so

speaker: AS
words: we got out here and so some of 'em they was telling us that we had

speaker: AS
words: to watch out for these wild animals; course we didn t know what they

speaker: AS
words: looked like." So they was clearing brush, clearing out an area for

speaker: AS
words: the camp and mostly just the small brush, and of course, they give

speaker: AS
words: 'em an axe apiece and they were chopping down these bushes and I

speaker: AS
words: guess the bushes were falling down around his feet, you know, and

speaker: AS
words: finally he said he seen something wiggle down in there, you know,

speaker: AS
words: and he thought that was one of them wild beasts and he let fly with

speaker: AS
words: the axe and he chopped his own foot! But there was a fellow here

speaker: AS
words: by the name of Ivor Ross, he was a carpenter that worked down here

speaker: AS
words: at the plant for a long time and he hired on as a camp carpenter

speaker: AS
words: when they was building these CC camps. And he had some men and he

speaker: AS
words: had to construct these bottoms for these tents, you know, they built

speaker: AS
words: a floor and sidewalls on it and then, of course, canvas top. And

speaker: AS
words: he said that in clearing these areas, said there was some of them

speaker: AS
words: guys- they had to issue handsaws to 'em for cutting this little bitty

speaker: AS
words: brush, you know, and said they didn't dare give 'em an axe, he said

speaker: AS
words: they was chopping themselves and chopping each other and everything

speaker: AS
words: else.

speaker: AS
words: They didn't know what an axe was, so they give 'em handsaws

speaker: AS
words: to cut them little bushes off, you know. Well, anyway, I was working

speaker: AS
words: in the powerhouse and they was prorating the labor in the powerhouse

speaker: AS
words: as to make it go as far as they could so I was working four hours

speaker: AS
words: a day and I think about four days a week, getting in about sixteen

speaker: AS
words: hours. But anyway, I was working in the powerhouse there when a

speaker: AS
words: bunch of these CCCs come down there and they had somebody with

speaker: AS
words: 'em for a guide and was showing them the plant. Of course the plant

speaker: AS
words: was all down, but then they could come in and look at this big engine

speaker: AS
words: and they could look at these fires and that sort of thing. And they

speaker: AS
words: could see anything else in the plant they was interested in, but

speaker: AS
words: they was mostly young men^above average intelligence, you know, be

speaker: AS
words: cause they showed interest in something like that, so I was talking

speaker: AS
words: with one young fellow there, he was a big, tall fellow; pretty goodlooking

speaker: AS
words: young fellow and I asked him where he was from and he said

speaker: AS
words: he was from New York. So I said, "What did you do in New York? What

speaker: AS
words: did you work at before you come out here?" Oh, he looked at me kind

speaker: AS
words: of funny and I said, "Did you have a job?" "No." "Well," I said,

speaker: AS
words: "how in the world did you get by?" He said just as long as he could

speaker: AS
words: remember, from the time he was born, all he ever knew was just to go

speaker: AS
words: down and get in the soupline every day, and work their way up to

speaker: AS
words: where they could get the chunk of bread and a bowl of soup. That's

speaker: AS
words: all he knew all his life until he come out here. And he was a grown

speaker: AS
words: up man. So it was really tough back in the big cities. Of course,

speaker: AS
words: he was growing up and he was in that soupline long before the Dep

speaker: AS
words: ression hit. He was probably, oh, maybe nineteen, twenty years old.

speaker: SS
words: What happened to these men who did^ have jobs, let s say the guys

speaker: SS
words: that worked on the saw? People who didn't have a job where they

speaker: SS
words: could just work in a powerplant or something that was necessary to

speaker: SS
words: maintain the mill? They didn't have any work or did the company

speaker: SS
words: make work for them?

speaker: AS
words: They made work for some of 'em, but they couldn't make work for all

speaker: AS
words: of 'em. Now there was- They started a WPA; they had those projects

speaker: AS
words: started after Roosevelt got in and so this highway from Moscow going

speaker: AS
words: towards Coeur d'Alene; most of that highway was built at that time,

speaker: AS
words: with WPA labor. And it was more or less hand labor. They had some

speaker: AS
words: machinery, but they used a lot of horses and fresnoes and slipscrapers

speaker: AS
words: And the men that worked on that highway, they were the same way, they

speaker: AS
words: got just four hours a day for so many days a week, you know, so as

speaker: AS
words: to spread it over as many as they could. And most everybody had some

speaker: AS
words: work of some kind. Now you couldn't be choosey on the work. If a

speaker: AS
words: job was offered to you, why, you'd better take it, if it would be

speaker: AS
words: dirty as the dickens or anything else. And there was one project

speaker: AS
words: that the company made was they put a new roof on this water reservoir

speaker: AS
words: for the town up here at the end of this street. And it's a big con

speaker: AS
words: crete reservoir, it holds about a million gallons of water, but the

speaker: AS
words: roof covers a pretty big area. Well, they refoofed that and of

speaker: AS
words: course, the company furnished the material. the

speaker: AS
words: company owned it. But for carpenters on the job, carpenters got

speaker: AS
words: paid twenty-seven cents an hour. And that's what we got paid down

speaker: AS
words: at the mill; twenty-seven cents an hour. Well, that's sure a far cry

speaker: AS
words: from what they're getting now! But of course, if a man earned a

speaker: AS
words: dollar, why, he could buy quite a bit with that dollar, you know,

speaker: AS
words: you could buy, oh, for half of that you could buy a pretty nice

speaker: AS
words: piece of meat and a sack of spuds would be about fifty cents. All

speaker: AS
words: things in proportion, people weathered the storm pretty well. Of

speaker: AS
words: course, nobody made any money.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the company had to make any hard choices about who they

speaker: SS
words: were going to be able to support or not?

speaker: AS
words: Well, it was- One thing that they did do, they definitely wanted to

speaker: AS
words: keep all what might be considered key men here. They didn't want

speaker: AS
words: them to get away. You take men of skill in the production of lumber;

speaker: AS
words: you take the filers in the sawmill, they filed the saws, the sawyers

speaker: AS
words: in the sawmill, the edgermen, setters, lumber graders and some of

speaker: AS
words: the. top mechanics, they wanted all them to stay and they wanted to

speaker: AS
words: provide them enough work so that they would stay. Now you take just

speaker: AS
words: base labor, common labor, they was down at the end of the totem pole,

speaker: AS
words: because if and when the time come when they'd get to operating again

speaker: AS
words: why, those jobs could be filled a lot easier than these jobs that re

speaker: AS
words: quired a lot of skill, you see. But even those of just common labor

speaker: AS
words: I don't know of any of 'em that- but what they got some work some

speaker: AS
words: where, you know. If they didn't work here they probably worked on

speaker: AS
words: that road or someplace, you know. And pretty near everybody cut

speaker: AS
words: their own wood. Those days we all burned wood in their kitchen

speaker: AS
words: ranges and in our heating stoves and we could get all the timber we

speaker: AS
words: wanted for nothing.

speaker: AS
words: All we had to do was cut it. Of course, we had

speaker: AS
words: to provide transportation for hauling it home, but I know there was

speaker: AS
words: a lot of cooperation there, too, even in making wood there. Most

speaker: AS
words: everybody that cut wood, they would cut in four foot lengths

speaker: AS
words: and haul it home and pile it up. Well then, there was oh, two or three

speaker: AS
words: or possibly individuals that had buzzsaws; an old automobile

speaker: AS
words: they'd converted over into a wood cutting outfit, you know. Took the

speaker: AS
words: body off it and just had the running gear and they'd get ahold of

speaker: AS
words: a saw^and so whenever they d go to cut somebody s wood, why, that per

speaker: AS
words: son that owned the wood he would get-some of his friends or neigh

speaker: AS
words: bors to come over and help. Well, you know, it wasn't too big a trick

speaker: AS
words: to cut forty cords a day because with one man or two men bringing the

speaker: AS
words: wood up to the saw and they generally moved that saw, kept it right

speaker: AS
words: up to the woodpile, one man actually sawing the wood and another man

speaker: AS
words: off bearing, you know taking these chumks as they come off

speaker: AS
words: the saw and throwing them out of the way, why, they'd probably cut

speaker: AS
words: ten cords for you and over and cut maybe ten cords for me and

speaker: AS
words: maybe ten cords for somebody else in a day. But the only one that

speaker: AS
words: got any pay was the man that owned the saw; he got so much a cord

speaker: AS
words: for cutting the wood. The rest of the help is all cooperative, one

speaker: AS
words: would help the other one, you see. But generally in making the wood

speaker: AS
words: out in the woods, usually it was a one-man deal there, sometimes two

speaker: AS
words: would go together, and if they was using a crosscut saw then the two

speaker: AS
words: men would work together and they'd divide the wood^they made. But

speaker: AS
words: there wasn't any power saws in the woods, it was all crosscut saws.

speaker: SS
words: Did the company give preference to married men over single men as far

speaker: SS
words: as helping?

speaker: AS
words: Yeah. They did, except if there was some single men that held jobs

speaker: AS
words: of importance; then of course, that single man. he was given the same

speaker: AS
words: consideration. But they were pretty fair about keeping families to

speaker: AS
words: gether. I think they done a real good job of administering the whole

speaker: AS
words: thing. After all, it was pretty difficult times for everybody.

speaker: SS
words: If you were going to adait up and look at the advantages and dis

speaker: SS
words: advantages of a company town, how do you think they would weigh out?

speaker: SS
words: I mean, I'm not saying that the imbalance is going to be terrific

speaker: SS
words: or it's going to be bad, but what do you think would be the advantages

speaker: SS
words: and the disadvantages of it?

speaker: AS
words: Well, somebody listening to these tapes, if they ever do in the future,

speaker: AS
words: and you put a question like that to me about the advantages and dis

speaker: AS
words: advantages of a company town, you've got to take into account there's

speaker: AS
words: a lot of differences- there might be a lot of differences in a company

speaker: AS
words: town.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I guess I happened to be thinking of this one.

speaker: AS
words: But .this one here; oh,'you take- I think in every aspect of the town,

speaker: AS
words: the people were better off as a company town than they are in an open

speaker: AS
words: town because of the fact that the company maintained and they done

speaker: AS
words: a good job of maintaining all the facilities and all the buildings

speaker: AS
words: and everything and there was no added expense to any of the people

speaker: AS
words: that lived here. When a person rented a house; well houserent run

speaker: AS
words: from about eight dollars a month up. And I guess probably the top

speaker: AS
words: rent for some of the best houses in town probably run up to maybe

speaker: AS
words: thirty-five dollars a month. But when you stop and consider that

speaker: AS
words: the streets, sidewalks, the buildings inside and out, the schools,

speaker: AS
words: the churches, everything was maintained and paid for entirely by

speaker: AS
words: the company and you didn't have to pay for any of that, you was bound

speaker: AS
words: to be better off in a company town than you was in owning it yourself.

speaker: AS
words: The only thing is that when a person gets to retirement age, well,

speaker: AS
words: even when you retired^1 have to pay the same taxes and the same in

speaker: AS
words: surance and the same maintenance cost as person would that's thirtyfive

speaker: AS
words: years old. There's no difference. I pay the same taxes as any

speaker: AS
words: body else would that's paying taxes for evaluation. So moneywise,

speaker: AS
words: why, you're bound to lose money by owning it. The only thing is,

speaker: AS
